question: Explain the difference between IRQ and FIQ in ARM architecture.
expectedPoints: IRQ is standard priority interrupt, FIQ is high-priority fast interrupt
FIQ has dedicated banked registers (r8-r14) unlike IRQ
FIQ has lower latency (4 cycles) compared to IRQ (20+ cycles)
FIQ can preempt IRQ handling but IRQ cannot preempt FIQ
FIQ has private vector address at 0x0000001C

question: Describe the ARM exception vector table and list all exception vectors in order.
expectedPoints: Vector table contains branch instructions to exception handlers
Located at 0x00000000 or 0xFFFF0000 (configurable)
Order: Reset(0x00), Undefined(0x04), SWI(0x08), Prefetch Abort(0x0C), Data Abort(0x10), IRQ(0x18), FIQ(0x1C)
Each vector typically contains branch instruction to handler
FIQ last to allow direct handler placement

question: Explain the sequence of operations when an exception occurs in ARM processor.
expectedPoints: 1. CPSR is copied to SPSR of the appropriate mode
2. Return address is saved in LR (R14) of the exception mode
3. CPSR is updated: set to exception mode, disable interrupts if needed
4. PC is set to exception vector address
5. Handler executes at vector location
6. Return using SUBS PC,LR,#4 or MOVS PC,SPSR

question: What are banked registers in ARM? Explain their significance in exception handling.
expectedPoints: Banked registers are private copies available in different processor modes
Each exception mode (IRQ, FIQ, SVC, etc.) has its own banked registers
FIQ has 7 banked registers (r8-r14_fiq) allowing fast context switch
IRQ has banked registers r13-r14 for stack and return address
Eliminates need to save/restore general registers to memory, reducing latency

question: How do you return from an IRQ exception in ARM? Explain with example instructions.
expectedPoints: Use SUBS PC, LR, #4 to return from IRQ handler
This instruction restores CPSR from SPSR (S suffix) and adjusts return address
The #4 adjustment accounts for ARM's 3-stage pipeline
Alternative: POP {PC} after restoring SPSR to CPSR manually
For FIQ also use SUBS PC, LR, #4 after restoring banked registers

question: Explain the role of CPSR and SPSR in exception handling.
expectedPoints: CPSR (Current Program Status Register) holds current processor state
SPSR (Saved Program Status Register) is banked for each privileged mode
On exception entry, CPSR is copied to SPSR of the respective mode
CPSR is updated with new mode bits and interrupt disable flags
On exception return, SPSR is restored to CPSR to restore original state

question: What is interrupt latency? Compare IRQ and FIQ latency in ARM7TDMI.
expectedPoints: Interrupt latency = time from interrupt request to first instruction execution
ARM7TDMI IRQ latency: typically 10-20 clock cycles (pipeline flush + vector fetch)
FIQ latency: typically 4 cycles due to banked registers and dedicated vector
FIQ can be placed directly at vector address avoiding branch delay
Factors affecting latency: current instruction, pipeline state, memory wait states

question: Describe in detail the exception handling mechanism in ARM architecture with special emphasis on IRQ and FIQ. Include vector addresses, registers used, and return mechanism.
expectedPoints: Exception vector table locations and entries
Exception modes: 7 modes with banked registers
IRQ handling: banked r13_irq, r14_irq, SPSR_irq, sequence
FIQ handling: banked r8-r14_fiq, lower latency, private vector
CPSR changes: I/F bits for interrupt disable
SPSR save/restore for state preservation
Return mechanism: SUBS PC,LR,#4 for IRQ, MOVS PC,SPSR
Priority: FIQ > IRQ > other exceptions
Nesting considerations and enable/disable methods

question: Design an FIQ handler in ARM assembly language. Explain how FIQ achieves better performance than IRQ with reference to banked registers and vector placement.
expectedPoints: FIQ handler structure: save only required registers if any
Banked registers r8-r14 available without save/restore
Direct placement at 0x1C avoiding branch instruction
FIQ disable using CPSID F or setting F bit in CPSR
Example code structure with context save only if needed
CPSR restoration and return using SUBS PC,LR,#4
Performance comparison: 4 cycles FIQ vs 20+ cycles IRQ
Design considerations: keep FIQ handler short, avoid blocking

question: Compare and contrast IRQ and FIQ interrupts in ARM microcontrollers under the following headings: (a) Priority (b) Number of interrupts (c) Latency (d) Banked registers (e) Vector address.
expectedPoints: (a) Priority: FIQ has higher priority, can preempt IRQ
(b) Number: FIQ typically 1 line, IRQ can have multiple with controller
(c) Latency: FIQ ~4 cycles, IRQ ~20+ cycles in ARM7
(d) Banked registers: FIQ has 7 (r8-r14), IRQ has 2 (r13-r14)
(e) Vector: FIQ at 0x1C (last), IRQ at 0x18
Additional: FIQ can disable IRQ during execution, IRQ cannot
Use cases: FIQ for time-critical (DMA, timers), IRQ for general devices

question: Explain the complete process of interrupt nesting in ARM. Discuss when nesting is used and what precautions are necessary when nesting IRQ handlers.
expectedPoints: Nesting allows higher priority interrupt to interrupt lower priority
IRQ nesting: re-enable IRQ within handler by clearing I bit in CPSR
Stack management: must save LR, SPSR, and required registers before re-enabling
Careful with data corruption between nested handlers
FIQ can always preempt IRQ; IRQ can preempt FIQ only if FIQ re-enabled
Return sequence must properly restore nested context
Performance trade-off: nesting adds overhead but improves responsiveness
Alternative: use priorities in interrupt controller to avoid nesting
